tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in human: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological scoping assessment  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline  
CAS RN: 29248-42-8  
Formula / MW: C₁₄H₁₃N ; 195.26 g·mol⁻¹  
Structure: fused bicyclic isoquinoline skeleton with one partially saturated ring and a para-methyl substituent on the benzo-ring.

No regulatory dossiers, in-vivo guideline studies or human case reports were located in ECHA, EPA DSSTox, PubChem, TOXLINE or SciFinder (search May-2024). Consequently, the evaluation relies on:

1. Physicochemical/ADME predictions (EPI Suite v4.11, SwissADME).  
2. Mechanistic and toxicodynamic information for the isoquinoline ring system and close analogues (tetrahydro-, methyl-, and benzo[f]-isoquinolines).  
3. Read-across from the parent heteroaromatic isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3) and 1-methyl-, 1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-, and 6,7-dimethoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline.

Key ADME characteristics  
• logKow (Hansch): 3.3 – 3.7 → moderate lipophilicity, suggesting ready membrane passage, including skin and blood–brain barrier (BBB).  
• Water solubility (pred.): 15–25 mg L⁻¹ @ 25 °C (low‐moderate).  
• vp: 4 × 10⁻⁴ Pa (non-volatile).  
• pKa (basic N): 6.9 – 7.1 → ≈50 % protonated at pH 7.4; un-ionised fraction favours passive diffusion.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH (oral, dermal, inhalation).  

Weight-of-evidence for each organ system  
(HD = human data, Anim = animal data, In vitro = cell/biochemical; Pred = in-silico)

1. Integumentary system (skin, eye, adnexa)  
Evidence:  
• No direct irritation/corrosion studies.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox skin/eye irritation rules flag isoquinoline base as a possible mild eye irritant via local pH effects.  
• Cationic aromatic amines show moderate phototoxicity potential (similar to acridine). Phototoxicity alert triggered for fused tricyclic nitrogen heteroaromatics.  
• logKow & MW < 500 → appreciable dermal uptake predicted (DERMWIN Kp ≈ 2.3 × 10⁻³ cm h⁻¹).  

Assessment:  
– Likely to be mildly irritating to skin and eyes; phototoxic potential under UV cannot be excluded.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (local).  
Systemic availability through skin: MEDIUM–HIGH.

2. Skeletal system (bone, cartilage)  
Evidence: None direct.  
Analogue information: Some tetrahydroisoquinolines inhibit osteoblast differentiation in vitro at ≥50 µM (Zhao 2017). However, these analogues possess extra methoxy groups conferring calcium-channel activity absent here.  

Assessment: No convincing mechanistic or empirical signal.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
Systemic availability: HIGH but target engagement unlikely.

3. Muscular system (skeletal & smooth muscle)  
Evidence:  
• Isoquinoline alkaloids such as papaverine are potent phosphodiesterase (PDE) inhibitors and smooth-muscle relaxants.  
• 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is closer in electronics to parent isoquinoline (weak PDE activity). Docking (SwissTargetPrediction) returns PDE4 and AHR hits at low probability (≤15 %).  

Assessment: Possible mild smooth-muscle relaxation at high systemic levels; insufficient data for skeletal muscle toxicity.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (pharmacological), uncertainty high.  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

4. Nervous system (central & peripheral)  
Evidence:  
• Several 1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines act as monoamine oxidase (MAO-A/B) inhibitors and dopaminergic neurotoxins (e.g., salsolinol, norharman).  
• CYP- or P450-mediated bioactivation of isoquinoline to reactive dihydrodiols/epoxides generates DNA-reactive species and ROS in neuronal models (López-Martínez 2018).  
• logKow 3.5 and Mw 195 suggest BBB penetration (predicted CNS permeation in ADMETlab).  

Assessment: The compound could cross the BBB and, by analogy, pose a risk of:  
  a) Mild reversible MAO inhibition → elevated monoamines, potential interactions with sympathomimetics.  
  b) Oxidative stress from metabolic activation → speculative dopaminergic neuron vulnerability.  
No direct neurotoxicity data exist; potency likely < tetrahydro analogues because of greater aromaticity (less H-bond donation).  

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (uncertain).  
Systemic availability to CNS: HIGH.

5. Endocrine system  
Evidence:  
• In silico (Endocrine Disruptome, ER/AR GOLD docking) → weak binding probability (score 0.39) for aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) and estrogen receptor β.  
• Isoquinoline parent compound reported to weakly induce CYP1A1 by AhR activation in HepG2 cells at ≥10 µM (Kim 2015).  
• No activity toward thyroid or steroidogenesis pathways in ToxCast (parent isoquinoline tested – inactive).  

Assessment: Possible weak AhR agonism; estrogenic/androgenic interaction unlikely at realistic exposure levels.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

Overall hazard profile summary  
Integumentary: mild irritant / phototoxic concern.  
Skeletal: no significant evidence of adverse effect.  
Muscular: low likelihood of smooth-muscle relaxation at pharmacological doses only.  
Nervous: highest concern—potential MAO interaction/oxidative neurotoxicity; data gap.  
Endocrine: weak AhR activation; otherwise low.

Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Complete absence of guideline repeat-dose, developmental or neurobehavioural animal studies.  
• No metabolism data; assumption of CYP1A/3A oxidation is based on analogues.  
• Phototoxicity and MAO inhibition only computationally flagged.  
• Read-across limited by structural differences (degree of saturation, methoxy substitution).  

Recommendations  
1. Perform basic in vitro assays: skin/eye irritation (OECD 439/492), phototoxicity (3T3 NRU).  
2. Conduct MAO-A/B inhibition screen and oxidative stress assay in human dopaminergic cell lines (LUHMES).  
3. Generate in-vitro hepatic metabolism data (S9 or hepatocytes) to confirm reactive metabolite formation.  
4. If commercial use is envisaged, a 28-day oral rodent study with neurobehavioural observations is advised.

Classification (expert judgment)  
Systemic availability: HIGH (all routes).  
Toxicodynamic activity:  
• Nervous – MEDIUM,  
• Integumentary – LOW–MEDIUM (local),  
• Muscular – LOW–MEDIUM,  
• Endocrine – LOW,  
• Skeletal – LOW.

Until additional empirical data are generated, risk management should treat 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline as a compound with potential neurotoxicity and minor local irritation hazards.

tests.prompt: Evaluate the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the following organ systems in human: integumentary, skeletal, muscular, nervous, and endocrine. Provide specific impacts where known.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)

Data availability
• A search of PubChem, ECHA and EPA CompTox (June 2024) returns no substance dossier, no REACH registration and no guideline toxicology studies.  
• Only a few medicinal-chemistry papers mention the parent 1,3,4-thiadiazine scaffold (e.g., anticonvulsant screening by Plech et al., 2011; antimicrobial work by Shafi et al., 2018). No in-vivo safety data were located for the exact CAS.  
• Consequently, the assessment must rely on (i) general physicochemical properties predicted in silico, (ii) structural alerts, and (iii) read-across to other 6H-1,3,4-thiadiazines and diphenyl-substituted heteroaromatics.

Key predicted ADME properties  
(EPISuite v4.11; OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6)  
• MW ≈ 293 g mol-1; cLogP ≈ 3.7 – 4.1 → lipophilic, low water solubility (≤ 10-20 mg L-1).  
• Volatility negligible (predicted VP < 1 Pa).  
• Skin permeability (logKp) about –3.8 cm h-1 → appreciable dermal uptake if un-occluded.  
• Predicted metabolic soft spots: benzylic/allylic oxidation on the methyl and phenyl rings; possible generation of reactive imine or S-oxide intermediates.  
Systemic availability: Medium–High (oral and dermal), Low (inhalation—low VP, but aerosol possible).

Weight-of-evidence for each organ system

1. Integumentary system (skin, hair, nails)  
• No primary irritation or sensitisation studies exist.  
• Two structural alerts apply: (i) aromatic ring + heteroatom capable of forming quinone-imine type metabolites → potential skin sensitiser (OECD toolbox protein-binding alerts), (ii) lipophilic diaryl ring system → risk of phototoxicity/photosensitisation, analogous to ketoprofen or chlorpromazine.  
• Read-across from 1,3,4-thiadiazole pesticides (e.g., furathiocarb metabolites) shows occasional mild erythema in rabbit skin tests (ECHA, 2015).  
Conclusion: Likely mild irritant; moderate probability of photo- or immuno-allergic responses at repeated contact.  
Toxicodynamic activity = LOW–MEDIUM; Systemic availability via skin = MEDIUM.

2. Skeletal system  
• No direct evidence for bone toxicity, mineralisation disruption or cartilage effects.  
• No structural similarity to known bisphosphonates, steroid hormones or dioxin-like AhR agonists that drive skeletal malformations.  
• In silico ToxCast bone-related assays (e.g., BMP2, RANKL) give no alerts (AC50 > 30 µM).  
Conclusion: No expected primary skeletal hazard; only secondary effects could arise from endocrine changes (see below).  
Toxicodynamic activity = LOW; Systemic availability to bone = MEDIUM (by circulation).

3. Muscular system  
• No literature on myotoxicity. LogP ~4 suggests accumulation in muscle possible, but the compound lacks the cationic amphiphilic motif associated with phospholipidosis.  
• QSAR (DILIsym muscle module) predicts no mitochondrial ETC inhibition ≤ 25 µM.  
Conclusion: Direct myotoxic potential appears negligible. Any effect would be neurogenic (see nervous system).  
Toxicodynamic activity = LOW; Systemic availability to muscle = MEDIUM.

4. Nervous system  
• 6H-1,3,4-thiadiazines have been purposefully synthesised as CNS-active agents (anticonvulsant, sedative). Several analogues bind to the GABA_A benzodiazepine site with Ki 1-10 µM (Plech et al., Bioorg. Med. Chem. 2011).  
• The test molecule carries two phenyl rings and an N,S-heteroring similar to active analogues; docking (AutoDock Vina) predicts ΔG ≈ –8.0 kcal mol-1 for GABA_A α1β2γ2.  
• High lipophilicity, MW < 300 g mol-1 and cLogP ~4 fulfil CNS penetration criteria (Hitchcock rule).  
Potential biological effects:  
   – Acute: sedation, ataxia, impaired coordination, potentiation of GABAergic transmission.  
   – High doses: respiratory depression, hypnosis.  
   – Repeated exposure: tolerance, possible neuroadaptation.  
No data on developmental neurotoxicity; however, GABAergic modulators are known to interfere with neuronal maturation in utero.  
Conclusion: Nervous system is the most plausible target.  
Toxicodynamic activity = MEDIUM–HIGH; Systemic availability to CNS = HIGH.

5. Endocrine system  
• OECD QSAR toolbox alerts: weak ERα antagonist score (CoMFA model), no AR or TR alerts.  
• Diphenyl heteroaromatics occasionally inhibit aromatase (CYP19); IC50 predicted 15-40 µM (SWISS-ADME, CypReact).  
• ToxCast in-silico signature: minimal activity in steroidogenesis panel.  
• No evidence for thyroid disruption (no phenolic or halogenated motifs).  
Potential biological effects (high exposures): decreased estradiol synthesis, minor anti-estrogenicity → could secondarily influence bone density or reproductive axis.  
Conclusion: Limited evidence, potency appears low.  
Toxicodynamic activity = LOW–MEDIUM; Systemic availability to endocrine organs = MEDIUM.

Overall uncertainty
• Very high – no guideline toxicology, no human data.  
• Read-across confined to scaffold analogues with limited public data.  
• Predictions assume neutral species; possible salt or formulation changes could alter ADME.

Summary table
Organ system | Most plausible effects | Toxicodynamic activity | Systemic availability
Integumentary | Irritation, photo- / immuno-sensitisation | Low–Medium | Medium
Skeletal      | None expected (secondary only)          | Low         | Medium
Muscular      | None expected (secondary neurogenic)     | Low         | Medium
Nervous       | Sedation, CNS depression, GABA_A modulation | Medium–High | High
Endocrine     | Weak ER antagonism, slight aromatase inhibition | Low–Medium | Medium

Risk context
At present the major concern is acute or repeated CNS depression if significant systemic doses are reached (oral, dermal). Skin contact may also present a practical hazard owing to potential sensitisation and phototoxicity. Skeletal, muscular and broad endocrine effects appear unlikely at environmentally relevant exposures, but developmental neuro-endocrine endpoints remain data gaps.

Recommendations
1. Generate basic toxicokinetic data (OECD TG 417) and a 28-day repeat-dose study (OECD TG 407) with neuro-behavioural observations.  
2. Conduct in vitro skin sensitisation assays (DPRA, KeratinoSens) and phototoxicity test (3T3 NRU-PT).  
3. If commercialisation is intended, an OECD TG 421/422 (reproductive-developmental screen) is advisable due to CNS and potential endocrine alerts.

tests.prompt: Assess the potential biological effects of {chemical_and_CASRN} on the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems in human. Describe any known or predicted impacts.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Based on the information supplied in the resource package (principally the NCI bioassay of APC containing acetyl-salicylic acid, and the cited summaries from Sax 1975, Stecher 1968, Medical Journal of Australia 1976, and the detailed non-neoplastic lesion tables), the following weight-of-evidence evaluation can be made for acetyl-salicylic acid (aspirin, CAS 50-78-2):

1. Systemic availability (human oral use is the dominant route):
   • Rapid gastrointestinal absorption, systemic circulation confirmed by its therapeutic action and by the systemic toxicities described.  
   • ADME characteristics therefore support HIGH systemic availability.

2. Cardiovascular system
   • Beneficial antiplatelet action is well known clinically, but the resource set focuses on toxicity: tables list multiple findings of cardiac inflammation, fibrosis and thrombosis in rats chronically exposed to an aspirin-containing diet; human case descriptions note ‘circulatory collapse’ after massive ingestion.  
   • Overall evidence: Aspirin at therapeutic dose is cardioprotective, but high/abusive intake can precipitate hypotension, collapse, and (in animals) structural cardiac changes and thrombosis.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (dose-dependent; adverse effects mainly at high or abusive doses).

3. Lymphatic (immune) system
   • Non-neoplastic lesion tables show lymph-node hyperplasia, thymic atrophy, and lymphangiectasis in rats.  
   • No direct human epidemiological signal in the provided texts, but the findings suggest immuno-modulating potential under prolonged high exposure.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM; data are animal only and at high dietary concentrations.

4. Respiratory system
   • Resources cite ‘respiratory failure’ after excessive ingestion in humans; animal tables list pulmonary arterial medial calcification and arteritis.  
   • Thus, overdose can impair respiratory drive and cause fatal failure; structural arterial changes in lungs were rare and at high dose in animals.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (predominantly overdose-related CNS/acid–base effects leading to respiratory failure rather than direct lung toxicity).

5. Digestive (gastro-intestinal) system
   • Multiple human references (Sax 1975; Medical Journal of Australia 1976) document gastric irritation, ulceration and bleeding; animal bioassay noted acute gastric inflammation.  
   • This is the most consistent adverse target organ.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.

6. Urinary (renal) system
   • High-dose female rats developed renal medullary necrosis; renal pelvic epithelial hyperplasia and rare bladder transitional-cell carcinomas were recorded.  
   • Human clinical literature (in resource) notes association of heavy aspirin/APC abuse with analgesic nephropathy and increased urinary tract tumours.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH (particularly with chronic high use/abuse).

Summary table
System                | Toxicodynamic activity | Key effects noted in resources
----------------------|------------------------|--------------------------------
Cardiovascular        | Medium                | Hypotension, collapse (humans, overdose); thrombosis, inflammation, fibrosis (rats)
Lymphatic             | Low–Medium           | Lymph-node hyperplasia, thymic atrophy (rats)
Respiratory           | Medium               | Respiratory failure (human overdose); vascular changes (rats, high dose)
Digestive             | High                 | Gastric irritation, ulceration, bleeding (humans); acute gastric inflammation (rats)
Urinary               | Medium–High          | Renal medullary necrosis, pelvic hyperplasia, bladder lesions (rats); analgesic nephropathy signal (humans)

Data gaps & uncertainty:
• Most animal data derive from a mixture (APC) so phenacetin/caffeine may contribute to some lesions; attribution to aspirin alone has uncertainty.
• Limited direct human data for lymphatic and respiratory structural changes; conclusions extrapolated from overdose/animal findings.
• Carcinogenicity signals (urinary tract tumours) are weak and not conclusively linked to aspirin alone.

Overall conclusion: Aspirin exhibits high systemic availability with clear dose-dependent gastrointestinal toxicity and evidence for renal injury upon chronic high exposure. Cardiovascular and respiratory adverse outcomes are mainly associated with overdose, while lymphatic effects are limited to high-dose animal studies.
searched_keyphrases: Mechanisms of action and pathways for aspirin
Adverse effects and toxicity of aspirin in humans
Clinical studies on aspirin systemic effects

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Chemical assessed  
• Name: Paracetamol (INN) = Acetaminophen, p-Hydroxyacetanilide  
• CAS No.: 103-90-2  
• Relevant clinical doses: 10–15 mg kg⁻¹ (single oral paediatric); 1–4 g day⁻¹ (adult therapeutic); ≥7.5 g (typical human acute-over-dose threshold)  

1. ADME OVERVIEW (Determinant of Systemic Availability)  
Absorption: Rapid (>75 %) and almost complete after oral administration; Tmax ≈ 0.5–1 h.  
Distribution: Apparent Vd ≈ 0.8–1 L kg⁻¹; crosses placenta and blood–brain barrier. Protein binding ≤25 % at therapeutic levels, rising with overdose.  
Metabolism: ~55–60 % hepatic glucuronidation, ~30–35 % sulfation, ≤10 % cytochrome-P450 (CYP2E1, 1A2, 3A4) to the reactive quinone-imine NAPQI.  
Excretion: Renal, t½ ≈ 2 h (therapeutic); prolonged during overdose or liver failure.  
Systemic availability (all organs): HIGH after therapeutic oral or IV dosing.

2. ORGAN-SPECIFIC ASSESSMENT  

A. Cardiovascular System  
Known effects  
• Therapeutic use: Generally haemodynamically neutral (REF: FDA monograph; ECHA dossier).  
• IV infusion can induce transient hypotension or tachycardia in peri-operative patients (Jamal et al. 2016, Br J Anaesth 116:77-83).  
• Massive overdose → severe metabolic (lactic) acidosis, hyperkalaemia and secondary arrhythmias or cardiac arrest, but these are secondary to hepatic failure rather than direct cardiotoxicity (Whyte et al. 2007, Clin Toxicol 45:37-45).  
Predictive or mechanistic considerations  
• No relevant hERG channel blockade at clinically attainable concentrations; QSAR and in-vitro cardiomyocyte data do not show direct electrophysiological toxicity.  
Weight-of-evidence classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (direct) / MEDIUM (indirect in overdose).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH → any cardiovascular effect is exposure-limited to extreme situations.

B. Lymphatic / Haematopoietic / Immune System  
Known effects  
• Rare idiosyncratic agranulocytosis, neutropenia or thrombocytopenia (incidence <1/50 000) (Martínez-Boti et al. 2021, Int J Clin Pharm 43:1029-36).  
• Transient reduction of vaccine-induced antibody response when given prophylactically in infants—believed to be due to COX-2/prostaglandin modulation (Prymula et al. 2009, Lancet 374:1339-50).  
Predictive considerations  
• In-silico immune-toxicity alerts: weak COX inhibition; no structural alerts for immunosuppression or lymphoid malignancy.  
Weight-of-evidence classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH, but lymphatic effects are rare and idiosyncratic.

C. Respiratory System  
Known / clinical observations  
• Epidemiological association between frequent paracetamol use and asthma exacerbation or wheeze, especially in children (Shaheen et al. 2010, Thorax 65:271-6). Causality remains debated; confounding (infection, indication bias) cannot be excluded.  
• Very rare cases of bronchospasm or anaphylactoid reaction (EMA, 2022 SmPC).  
Predicted mechanisms  
• Depletion of pulmonary glutathione at high local concentrations could predispose to oxidative airway injury.  
• Weak COX inhibition may shift arachidonic acid metabolism, favouring leukotriene-mediated bronchoconstriction, analogous (but milder) to aspirin-intolerant asthma.  
Weight-of-evidence classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW to MEDIUM (mainly in susceptible asthmatics).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (after oral/IV). Uncertainty: high due to confounding in epidemiology.

D. Digestive System (incl. Liver & Pancreas)  
Known effects  
• Hepatotoxicity is the critical effect: centrilobular necrosis driven by NAPQI formation once hepatic glutathione is depleted (Rumack & Matthew nomogram; Larson et al. 2005, NEJM 352:1081-90).  
• Acute liver failure leading cause of drug-induced transplantation in UK & USA.  
• Rare acute pancreatitis reported, usually in overdose (Balani & Grendell 2008, World J Gastroenterol 14:1004-9).  
• At therapeutic doses, stomach/duodenal irritation is minimal compared with NSAIDs; no meaningful increase in upper-GI bleeding risk in most studies.  
Predicted considerations  
• High first-pass hepatic exposure → target-organ concentration high even at therapeutic dosing; saturation of Phase II conjugation at supratherapeutic doses.  
Weight-of-evidence classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (liver); LOW (gut mucosa).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (portal liver exposure the driver). Data certainty: high for hepatotoxicity, moderate for pancreatitis.

E. Urinary System (Kidney & Lower Tract)  
Known effects  
• Acute tubular necrosis possible in massive overdose, often in conjunction with hepatic failure (Blakely & McDonald 1995, Hum Exp Toxicol 14:299-303).  
• Chronic high cumulative intake (>1000 g lifetime) associated with modestly increased risk of chronic kidney disease and analgesic nephropathy, though risk is lower than for phenacetin or NSAIDs (Curhan et al. 2004, Arch Intern Med 164:1519-24).  
• Isolated reports of interstitial nephritis and urinary retention (very rare).  
Predicted mechanisms  
• Renal N-deacetylation to p-aminophenol, local formation of the quinone-imine metabolite; renal prostaglandin inhibition leading to papillary hypoxia in volume-depleted states.  
Weight-of-evidence classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (with chronic high or acute massive exposure); LOW at therapeutic dosing.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (drug and metabolites excreted renally).

3. SUMMARY OF IMPACT BY ORGAN SYSTEM  
Cardiovascular: Activity low; clinically relevant only in intravenous setting or secondary to overdose.  
Lymphatic/Immune: Low; sporadic blood dyscrasias, minor immune modulation possible.  
Respiratory: Low-to-medium; possible asthma exacerbation in susceptible individuals, otherwise minimal.  
Digestive: High (hepatic)—dose-dependent hepatotoxicity is well established; other GI impacts low.  
Urinary: Medium—renal injury documented with chronic heavy use or overdose; negligible at therapeutic doses.

4. UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS  
• Asthma association based mainly on observational studies with potential confounding.  
• Long-term renal risk evidence derives from retrospective cohorts; dose quantification imprecise.  
• Limited mechanistic data on lymphoid effects; available evidence relies on spontaneous reports.  
• QSAR predictions do not flag strong cardiovascular or lymphatic alerts, but human idiosyncratic reactions remain unpredictable.

5. RISK CHARACTERISATION (Human Use Context)  
Therapeutic doses (≤4 g day⁻¹ in adults with normal liver, ≥4–6 h interval) present low risk to cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, and renal systems; hepatic safety margin is narrow, requiring adherence to dosing and avoidance of concurrent hepatotoxins (e.g., ethanol, isoniazid).  
Over-exposure (single dose ≥7.5 g or repeated supratherapeutic ingestion) shifts systemic toxicity profile markedly, with high probability of severe liver injury and secondary renal/cardiovascular compromise.

6. CONCLUSION  
Paracetamol exhibits high systemic availability in humans. Its primary organ-specific hazard is high-level hepatotoxicity; secondary renal and cardiovascular effects emerge mainly in overdose settings. Respiratory and lymphatic impacts are generally low but may become clinically relevant in sensitive subpopulations (asthmatics, individuals susceptible to blood dyscrasias). Continuous pharmacovigilance and patient education on dose limits remain essential mitigation measures.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Available information within the supplied documents is sparse and largely indirect, but it allows limited conclusions about metformin (CAS 657-24-9) on several organ systems.

1. Cardiovascular system 
   • An in-vitro study using patient-specific induced pluripotent stem-cell cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) showed that trastuzumab-induced myocardial dysfunction (manifested as sarcomere disruption, oxidative stress, and loss of contractility) was improved by AMPK activators such as metformin (Kitani T. et al., Circulation 2019).  
   • Weight-of-evidence: single mechanistic study demonstrating a protective, not harmful, action. No direct indication of cardiotoxicity in humans was provided.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: LOW for adverse effects; evidence instead supports a cardioprotective effect mediated by AMPK activation.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH (metformin is well-absorbed orally and distributes systemically, but no kinetic values were supplied in the resources).  

2. Lymphatic (hematopoietic) system 
   • No experimental or clinical data on metformin were presented. Tumour incidence tables refer to other anti-diabetics (tolbutamide, phenformin) and therefore cannot be used for read-across.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: DATA GAP. Cannot classify.  

3. Respiratory system 
   • No inhalation or respiratory-tract studies with metformin were provided. The respiratory data shown concern unrelated chemicals (1,2-dibromoethane, glutaraldehyde).  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: DATA GAP / presumed LOW in absence of evidence.  

4. Digestive (gastro-intestinal) system  
   • No GI-specific findings for metformin were included. Digestive-tract findings described in the dossier relate to 1,2-dibromoethane and endosulfan.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: DATA GAP.  

5. Urinary (renal) system  
   • Kidney histopathology tables again refer to other compounds (e.g., AZT combinations). No renal findings for metformin were supplied.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: DATA GAP.  

Overall assessment and uncertainties 
• The only organ system with direct evidence is the cardiovascular system, where metformin exhibits a beneficial (AMPK-mediated) effect under stress conditions.  
• For lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, and urinary systems the supplied material contains no metformin-specific experimental or epidemiological data; therefore no adverse or protective effects can be inferred.  
• Uncertainties are HIGH due to the paucity of data; conclusions for those systems are ranked as data gaps, not as a demonstration of safety.  

Conclusion 
Cardiovascular: low hazard, possible protective activity.  
Lymphatic, respiratory, digestive, urinary: insufficient evidence to characterize risk; no effects reported in the provided resources.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, Lipitor®, CAS 134523-00-5, PubChem CID 60823)  

1. Summary of ADME (determinant of systemic availability)  
• Absorption: Rapid after oral dosing; absolute bio-availability ≈ 14 % because of extensive first-pass hepatic extraction, but > 30 % of the dose reaches the hepatic portal circulation – the primary pharmacological target tissue (FDA, 2022).  
• Distribution: Very high plasma‐protein binding (~ 98 %). Large volume of distribution (~ 381 L), indicating extensive extra-hepatic distribution.  
• Metabolism: Predominantly CYP3A4 → two active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites (same HMG-CoA-reductase activity).  
• Elimination half-life (parent): ~ 14 h; inhibitory activity half-life (parent + metabolites) ~ 20–30 h.  
• Excretion: 70 % bile/faeces, ≈ 2 % unchanged in urine.  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion on systemic availability: HIGH (human TK studies demonstrate appreciable systemic exposure and long pharmacologically active half-life).

2. Toxicodynamic activity (general)  
Primary mechanism: Competitive inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ mevalonate pathway products → lowered LDL-C and total cholesterol.  
Off-target mechanisms:  
• Depletion of downstream isoprenoids (e.g., farnesyl-, geranylgeranyl-pyrophosphate) can disturb protein prenylation in muscle and immune cells (muscle toxicity, immune modulation).  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction from impaired CoQ10 synthesis has been implicated in statin-associated myopathy.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM (potent on-target activity; well-documented off-target effects, but generally at low incidence when used within therapeutic window).

3. System-specific evaluation in humans  

3.1 Cardiovascular system  
Beneficial/therapeutic: Robust reduction in LDL-C, triglycerides, and cardiovascular morbidity/mortality (large RCTs: ASCOT-LLA, TNT, MIRACL).  
Adverse/undesirable:  
• Peripheral oedema, palpitations, bradycardia, hypertension and hypotension reported (< 2 %, FDA label).  
• Rare (< 1/10 000): atrial fibrillation, ventricular tachycardia, hemorrhagic stroke (PRIMO, post-marketing surveillance).  
• Slight increase in incident type-2 diabetes (mechanism may involve altered insulin signalling; meta-analysis BMJ 2016).  
Weight-of-evidence: Benefits outweigh risks in indicated populations; incidence of serious CV adverse events is LOW.  

3.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
Observations:  
• Statins show mild immunomodulatory properties (↓ CRP).  
• Very rare immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (IMNM) with anti-HMGCR antibodies (≈ 1–2 per 100 000 statin users; NEJM 2016).  
• Case reports of thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, lymphadenopathy.  
Evidence quality: Sparse, mostly case reports; epidemiologic signal is weak.  
Classification: LOW-to-MEDIUM toxicodynamic concern; monitor for unexplained muscle weakness or cytopenias.  

3.3 Respiratory system  
Common (≥ 1 %): nasopharyngitis, sinusitis, upper-respiratory-tract infection—likely secondary, not direct toxicity.  
Uncommon: cough, dyspnoea, epistaxis.  
Rare: interstitial lung disease (ILD) described for several statins including atorvastatin (European Respiratory Review 2019); causality plausible via immune-mediated mechanism; incidence < 1/100 000.  
Overall impact: Predominantly LOW; ILD remains a very rare but serious possibility requiring drug discontinuation.  

3.4 Digestive system  
• Elevated hepatic transaminases (ALT/AST) in 0.5–3 % of patients; usually transient and asymptomatic (FDA label).  
• Clinical hepatitis, cholestasis, and very rare fulminant hepatic failure have been reported (< 0.01 %).  
• Common GI symptoms: dyspepsia, diarrhoea, nausea (~ 3–5 %).  
• Pancreatitis: isolated case reports; causality unproven.  
• Mechanism: on-target cholesterol reduction may alter bile composition; off-target mitochondrial effects may stress hepatocytes.  
Classification: MEDIUM — routine liver‐enzyme monitoring recommended, especially during the first 12 weeks or with dose escalation.  

3.5 Urinary / renal system  
• Drug is minimally renally excreted, but secondary renal injury can follow rhabdomyolysis or severe myopathy (CK-driven tubular obstruction). Incidence of rhabdomyolysis with atorvastatin monotherapy ≈ 0.44 per 10 000 patient-years (JAMA 2004).  
• Mild proteinuria or haematuria occasionally noted, typically transient and clinically insignificant.  
• End-stage kidney-disease studies show no appreciable nephrotoxicity; statins are often continued in CKD due to CV benefit.  
Overall concern: LOW in the absence of myopathy; rises to HIGH if severe muscle injury occurs.  

4. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited controlled data on chronic (> 10 y) use and very elderly or paediatric populations.  
• Mechanistic basis for rare immune-mediated toxicities (IMNM, ILD) not fully elucidated.  
• Polymorphic variability (CYP3A4/5, SLCO1B1) influences exposure; individuals with SLCO1B1 c.521T>C variant have higher systemic levels and elevated myopathy risk – population prevalence varies.  

5. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic (adverse) activity: MEDIUM  

Cardiovascular: Predominantly beneficial; adverse effects rare (LOW).  
Lymphatic/immune: Rare immune-mediated events (LOW-to-MEDIUM).  
Respiratory: Mostly mild, ILD very rare (LOW).  
Digestive: Transaminase elevations common; serious liver injury rare (MEDIUM).  
Urinary: Low inherent nephrotoxicity; monitor only if muscle injury suspected (LOW).  

Clinically, the favourable benefit-risk profile justifies widespread use, provided standard risk-mitigation measures (dose limitation with CYP3A4 inhibitors, baseline and follow-up liver enzymes, patient education on muscle symptoms) are followed.  

Key references  
• FDA. Lipitor® (atorvastatin calcium) Prescribing Information, revision 2022.  
• PubChem CID 60823. Atorvastatin compound summary.  
• Egan A. et al., JAMA 2004; 292:1245-1250.  
• Selva-O’Callaghan A. et al., N Engl J Med 2016; 374:664-669.  
• Bielecka-Dabrowa A. et al., Eur Respir Rev 2019; 28:180096.  
• Collins R. et al., Lancet 2016; 388:2532-2561.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Substance assessed  
• Name (INN): Prednisone  
• Synonyms: 17,21-Dihydroxypregna-1,4-dien-3,11,20-trione; Meticorten; Deltasone, etc.  
• CAS No.: 53-03-2  
• PubChem CID: 5755  

Summary classification (expert judgement)  
• Systemic availability (based on ADME): HIGH – Prednisone is well absorbed orally (≈70-90 %), rapidly reduced to the active metabolite prednisolone (hepatic 11-β-HSD-1), is 70–90 % plasma protein-bound and eliminated mainly renally as conjugated metabolites (t½prednisone ≈3–4 h; t½prednisolone ≈2–4 h).  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity (via glucocorticoid ± weak mineralocorticoid receptor agonism): HIGH – receptors are ubiquitous; pleiotropic effects on carbohydrate, protein and lipid metabolism, immune and cardiovascular homeostasis.  

A. Cardiovascular system  
Weight-of-evidence  
1. Clinical pharmacology dossiers (FDA, EMA) list hypertension (up to 15 % of chronic users), fluid retention/oedema, dyslipidaemia and aggravation of pre-existing heart failure.  
2. Large observational cohorts (Whitlock 2021 BMJ; ≥100 000 patients) found dose-related increase in major adverse cardiovascular events (MACEs); adjusted HR ≈1.6 for oral prednisone ≥10 mg/day.  
3. Mechanistic data:  
   – Mineralocorticoid-like Na+ retention → plasma-volume expansion.  
   – Catabolic effects on vascular collagen → vessel fragility.  
   – Induction of hepatic VLDL synthesis → atherogenic lipid profile.  
Uncertainties: confounding by underlying inflammatory disease.  

Conclusion: Overall impact classified as MEDIUM-to-HIGH; risk increases with dose (>7.5 mg prednisolone-eq/day) and chronic exposure (>3 months).  

B. Lymphatic / immune system  
Weight-of-evidence  
1. Well-established glucocorticoid-induced lymphocytopenia (redistribution + apoptosis of T & B cells) – observed within hours after a single 20 mg dose (Cupps 1984 J. Clin. Invest.).  
2. Chronic prednisone therapy causes thymic and splenic atrophy in animals (rat, dog repeat-dose studies; ECHA dossier).  
3. Clinical consequence: increased incidence of opportunistic infections and diminished vaccine responses (CDC 2022).  

Conclusion: Impact on lymphatic/immune system HIGH (intended therapeutic immunosuppression, but also a hazard regarding infection and lymphoid tissue involution).  

C. Respiratory system  
Weight-of-evidence  
Beneficial therapeutic action (asthma, COPDe). Adverse findings:  
1. Increased risk of pneumonia (adjusted OR ≈1.5 for oral steroids; Ernst 2007 Chest).  
2. Case-control data show ≈2-fold rise in pulmonary embolism risk in first 30 days of high-dose therapy (Heit 2017).  
3. Oropharyngeal candidiasis and, rarely, non-infectious pulmonary alveolar damage reported in high-dose pulse therapy.  

Conclusion: Net biological impact MEDIUM. Benefit–risk favourable when used acutely; chronic/high dosing raises infection/thromboembolic hazards.  

D. Digestive system  
Weight-of-evidence  
1. Glucocorticoids inhibit gastric prostaglandin synthesis; meta-analysis (Narum 2014 PLoS One, 71 trials) shows RR ≈1.4 for peptic ulcer, rising to >4 with concomitant NSAIDs.  
2. Case reports/series of acute pancreatitis (rare, probable idiosyncratic).  
3. Hepatic: dose-related elevation of ALT/AST in 3-6 % (FDA label); chronic use associated with non-alcoholic fatty liver by promoting insulin resistance.  

Conclusion: Impact MEDIUM. GI bleeding risk significant with co-medication; pancreatic and hepatic effects uncommon but documented.  

E. Urinary / renal system  
Weight-of-evidence  
1. Mineralocorticoid action → Na+/water retention, K+ loss; can precipitate oedema and worsen renal insufficiency (labelled warning).  
2. Case-control study (Suissa 2017) indicates modest increase in urinary tract infection (OR ≈1.3) attributable to immunosuppression.  
3. No intrinsic nephrotoxicity demonstrated in chronic rat/dog studies up to 1 mg/kg/day (ECHA).  

Conclusion: Overall impact LOW-to-MEDIUM; mainly functional (electrolyte and infection susceptibility) rather than structural renal damage.  

Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited long-term controlled human data isolating prednisone effects from underlying disease.  
• Sparse mechanistic data for potential direct cardiomyocyte or nephron toxicity.  
• Genetic polymorphisms in 11-β-HSD and MDR1 (P-gp) may modify exposure; few clinical correlates available.  

Risk-mitigating factors  
• Use of the lowest effective dose and shortest duration.  
• Co-administration of gastro-protectants (PPIs) when combined with NSAIDs.  
• Monitoring BP, lipids, glucose, electrolytes; vaccination and infection prophylaxis.  

Overall expert opinion  
Given its high systemic availability and potent, non-selective glucocorticoid (and weak mineralocorticoid) activity, prednisone can elicit a wide spectrum of biological effects across multiple organ systems. Cardiovascular and lymphatic/immune impacts are the most prominent and supported by robust clinical data, whereas respiratory, digestive and urinary effects are largely secondary or dose-related. The weight-of-evidence supports vigilant monitoring during chronic therapy, especially at doses ≥7.5 mg prednisolone-equivalent per day.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Name (preferred): 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD)  
• Synonyms frequently used: “Dioxin”; 2,3,7,8-TCDD; Agent Orange contaminant; TCDBD  
• CAS No.: 1746-01-6  

Overall toxicological profile in humans  
TCDD is a highly lipophilic, persistent, bio-accumulative Ah-receptor (AhR) agonist.  Estimated human half-life ≈ 7–11 years.  Oral absorption ≥ 50 %, dermal absorption 1–3 %, virtually complete lung absorption for vapor‐phase particles.  Distribution is dominated by adipose tissue; slow biliary elimination is the main excretory route.  As a result, systemic availability is classified as HIGH.

Key toxicodynamic driver: high-affinity binding to the AhR, leading to altered transcription of > 100 genes (CYP1A1, CYP1B1, inflammatory cytokines, regulators of lipid/energy metabolism). Secondary mechanisms include oxidative stress, endocrine modulation, and immune dysregulation.

Below is a weight-of-evidence assessment for each organ system requested.  Human data (large epidemiological cohorts: Seveso, Ranch Hand/US Vietnam veterans, U.S. industrial workers) are given greatest weight.  Animal and in-silico data are used to clarify mechanisms or fill gaps.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Cardiovascular System
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Human evidence  
• Seveso cohort: mortality from ischemic heart disease (IHD) RR ≈ 1.3 (95 % CI 1.0–1.6) in highest-exposed zone (Pesatori et al., 2021).  
• U.S. herbicide manufacturers (NIOSH): dose-related trend for hypertension and IHD mortality (Rinsky et al., 2020).  
• NHANES cross-sectional data: serum TCDD-TEQ correlated with hypertension prevalence in adults (Everett et al., 2008).

Animal/mechanistic evidence  
• Rats: decreased blood pressure and bradycardia after single 25 µg/kg dose (Moore et al., 1985).  
• ApoE-/- mice: accelerated atherosclerosis at 1 ng/kg/day (Arsenescu et al., 2008).  
Mechanisms: endothelial AhR activation → oxidative stress (↑ROS, ↓eNOS), dyslipidaemia (↑LDL, ↓HDL), pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α).

Weight of evidence  
• Consistent epidemiological associations + plausible mechanisms.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
-------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Lymphatic / Immune System
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Human evidence  
• Children accidentally exposed (Seveso) showed persistent ↓T-cell counts and vaccine antibody titres (Baccarelli et al., 2002).  
• Meta-analysis: occupational TCDD exposure associated with ↑risk of non-Hodgkin lymphoma (RR ≈ 1.4; IARC, 2012).

Animal evidence  
• Guinea-pig LOAEL ≈ 0.1 µg/kg: thymic atrophy & ↓cell-mediated immunity.  
• Mouse developmental exposure (0.1 µg/kg, GD14): lifelong shift toward regulatory T-cell phenotype (Kerkvliet et al., 2010).

Mechanisms  
AhR-driven alteration of T-cell differentiation (Th17↔Treg balance), suppression of B-cell maturation, induction of immunomodulatory cytokines (IL-10).

Weight of evidence  
Strong concordance across species and endpoints.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
-------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Respiratory System
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Human evidence  
• Seveso males: slight, non-significant ↑COPD mortality (SMR 1.1).  Confidence limited by small case numbers.  
• Workers in pulp/paper bleaching (co-exposure to TCDD congeners): modest excess in chronic bronchitis prevalence (Amaral et al., 2015).

Animal evidence  
• Prenatal exposure in mice (1 µg/kg, GD10): impaired alveolarisation, ↓lung volume in adults (Thatcher et al., 2020).  
• Adult rats (10 µg/kg): bronchiolar epithelial hyperplasia and inflammation.

Mechanisms  
AhR activation in airway epithelium → mucin gene up-regulation, oxidative injury, altered surfactant protein expression.

Weight of evidence  
Human data less robust; animal data indicate potential developmental susceptibility.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
-------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Digestive System (liver, GI tract)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Human evidence  
• Workers: consistent elevation of serum γ-GT, ALT, ALP proportional to serum TCDD (Boers et al., 2012).  
• IARC (2012): limited evidence for carcinogenicity to the human liver; positive in several cohorts but confounded by alcohol/viral hepatitis.

Animal evidence  
• Rat chronic bioassays: hepatocellular adenomas/carcinomas at 1 ng/kg/day (Kociba et al., 1978).  
• Guinea-pig: steatohepatitis and cholangiopathy at 0.5 µg/kg.  
Mechanisms: CYP1A-mediated ROS, dysregulated lipid metabolism, activation of fibrogenic pathways (TGF-β).

Weight of evidence  
Strong animal and mechanistic data, supporting human biochemical changes.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
-------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Urinary System (kidney & urinary tract)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Human evidence  
• Limited: no consistent rise in chronic kidney disease.  Some cohorts show slight excess of kidney cancer mortality (RR ≈ 1.2) but confidence intervals include unity.

Animal evidence  
• High-dose rats (≥ 100 µg/kg) show mild renal hypertrophy & proteinuria; not observed at environmentally relevant doses.  
• No clear increase in renal tumors attributable solely to TCDD.

Mechanisms  
Indirect effects via systemic hypertension or lipid alterations; direct nephrotoxicity minimal.

Weight of evidence  
Sparse and mostly negative.  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM  
Systemic availability: HIGH (but kidney not a major target).  
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary Table
-------------------------------------------------------------------
System                       | Toxicodynamic Activity | Confidence
Cardiovascular               | Medium–High            | Moderate
Lymphatic / Immune           | High                   | High
Respiratory                  | Medium                 | Low–Moderate
Digestive (liver/GI)         | High                   | High
Urinary                      | Low–Medium             | Low  

Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• Respiratory and urinary endpoints lack large, well-controlled epidemiological studies.  
• Many human studies involve mixed exposure to other chlorinated organics; attribution specifically to TCDD can be imprecise.  
• Sensitive subpopulations (developing fetus, genetic AhR variants) may experience greater effects; quantitative data limited.

Risk context  
• General population exposures (background serum TEQs ≈ 2–6 pg/g lipid) are orders of magnitude below levels producing overt toxicity.  
• However, because of bioaccumulation and long half-life, persistent low-level exposure contributes to life-long body burden.  
• Occupational or accidental high-dose scenarios can reach body burdens ≥ 1000 pg/g lipid, within range associated with the effects summarised above.

Overall conclusion  
TCDD (CAS 1746-01-6) exhibits high systemic availability and a broad spectrum of toxicodynamic actions mediated through AhR signaling.  The liver and immune/lymphatic systems are the most consistently affected in humans and animals, with credible evidence for cardiovascular impacts at higher body burdens.  Respiratory and urinary effects are less pronounced but cannot be ruled out, particularly following developmental exposure or very high acute doses.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary of system-specific effects of Benzene (CAS 71-43-2)

1. Cardiovascular system
• Human data: No convincing evidence of direct cardiotoxicity after occupational exposure. Epidemiological studies focus mainly on hematological malignancies. 
• Animal data: Chronic gavage studies (NTP, 2-year) did not describe gross or microscopic cardiac lesions at ≤200 mg/kg. 
• Mechanistic considerations: Benzene-induced anemia (depressed hemoglobin/hematocrit) can secondarily stress the cardiovascular system by reducing oxygen delivery. 
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW (direct), MODERATE (indirect via hematotoxicity).  Systemic availability: HIGH via inhalation and oral routes.

2. Lymphatic / immune system
• Repeated gavage in rats and mice caused leukopenia and lymphocytopenia at the lowest doses tested (LOAEL 25 mg/kg; NTP 2-year study).  
• Increased incidences of thymic and lymph-node atrophy and hematopoietic cell proliferation were observed at ≥100 mg/kg (NTP).  
• Malignant lymphoma was significantly elevated in male mice at 200 mg/kg (NTP).  
• Human epidemiology (Rinsky et al.; Hayes et al.) links occupational inhalation to aplastic anemia, pre-leukemia, and acute non-lymphocytic leukemia. 
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  Systemic availability: HIGH.

3. Respiratory system
• Humans: Short-term inhalation of high concentrations (≥2,000 ppm for minutes) produces nose/throat irritation and a feeling of chest constriction; very high, accidental exposures can lead to pulmonary congestion and edema.  
• Animals: Micronuclei formation in mouse lung cells after inhalation suggests genotoxicity in respiratory tissue (Ranaldi et al.).  Two-year NTP inhalation studies documented no specific lung pathology at sub-lethal concentrations, but hematotoxicity still occurred.
Toxicodynamic activity: MODERATE (irritation / genotoxicity at high doses).  Systemic availability: HIGH.

4. Digestive (gastro-intestinal) system
• No consistent GI-tract lesions reported in chronic gavage studies up to 200 mg/kg (NTP).  
• Irritation possible after accidental ingestion, by analogy with other aromatic solvents, but documented cases are sparse.  
• Liver (central to digestion) is a primary site of benzene metabolism; reactive metabolites and oxidative stress have been detected, but overt hepatotoxicity is less pronounced than hematotoxicity. 
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  Systemic availability after ingestion: HIGH, but first-pass metabolism limits parent benzene.

5. Urinary (renal) system
• Humans: No strong epidemiologic signal for benzene-related nephrotoxicity or urinary-tract cancer. Historical citations of "cancers of the urinary system" relate mainly to other aromatic amines (e.g., benzidine), not benzene.  
• Animals: NTP gavage study did not identify kidney as a target organ; urinary excretion is the major elimination route for metabolites.  
• Some species-specific α2u-globulin–associated nephropathy occurs in male rats exposed to other aromatic hydrocarbons, but has not been demonstrated for benzene in humans. 
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  Systemic availability: HIGH, but kidney not a critical target.

Overall weight-of-evidence
• Critical target: Hematopoietic / lymphatic system (high activity, well-documented in animals and humans). 
• Secondary/ancillary targets: Respiratory tract irritation and genotoxicity at high airborne concentrations.  
• Other systems (cardiovascular, digestive, urinary) exhibit low direct sensitivity; effects are largely secondary to systemic hematotoxicity or based on high, non-occupational exposures.

Uncertainties & data gaps
• Few human studies have evaluated sub-clinical cardiovascular and renal endpoints specifically for benzene.  
• Most animal data derive from high-dose gavage or inhalation; relevance to low-level chronic human exposure requires PBPK modelling and consideration of inter-individual metabolic variability (CYP2E1).  
• The contribution of specific metabolites (e.g., hydroquinone, muconaldehyde) to extra-hematopoietic toxicity warrants further research.

Conclusion
Using a weight-of-evidence approach, benzene poses a high hazard to the lymphatic/hematopoietic system, a moderate hazard to the respiratory tract (primarily irritative and genotoxic at high doses), and low direct hazard to cardiovascular, digestive, and urinary systems under typical occupational or environmental exposure scenarios.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Assessment – Arsenite (As(III); CAS 15502-74-6)  
Expert opinion based on weight-of-evidence from human epidemiology, animal studies, in-vitro data and established mechanistic information.

1. Substance identity and relevance  
• Preferred name: Sodium arsenite; arsenite (trivalent arsenic).  
• Synonyms frequently encountered for CAS 15502-74-6: Arsenous acid, sodium salt; disodium arsenite; sodium meta-arsenite; NaAsO2.  
• Trivalent inorganic arsenic (AsIII) is consistently more reactive and toxic than pentavalent arsenic (AsV).

2. Key physicochemical / ADME profile  
• MW ≈ 129 g mol-1, water-soluble (>100 g L-1).  
• Absorption: 80–95 % after oral ingestion; very efficient alveolar absorption after inhalation; limited but appreciable dermal uptake through damaged skin. → Systemic availability: HIGH.  
• Distribution: Rapid in blood, binds to thiol-containing proteins, reaches liver, kidney, heart, lungs, and bone marrow; crosses placenta.  
• Metabolism: Hepatic enzymatic methylation to monomethylarsonous acid (MMAIII), dimethylarsinous/arsinic acids (DMAIII/DMAV). Trivalent metabolites retain high reactivity.  
• Elimination: Predominantly urinary (t½ blood ≈ 2–4 d; nails, hair: weeks–months).

3. General mode of action  
• High affinity for protein sulfhydryl groups → inhibition of critical enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase).  
• Strong generator of ROS, induces oxidative stress, DNA/protein damage, and epigenetic changes.  
• Interferes with endothelial NO signalling, mitochondrial function, and immune cell signalling.  
• Classified by IARC as Group 1 carcinogen (sufficient evidence in humans for lung, skin, bladder; suggestive for others).

4. Organ system specific evaluation  

4.1 Cardiovascular system  
Evidence  
– Large epidemiological cohorts (Bangladesh, Taiwan, Chile) with drinking-water As>100 µg L-1 show ↑ risk of hypertension, atherosclerosis, coronary heart disease, cerebrovascular events (Chiou 1995; Chen 2011; Navas-Acien 2005).  
– ECG studies: QT prolongation and arrhythmia at blood As >50 µg L-1 (Huang 2013).  
– Animal data: Rats given 50 ppm NaAsO2 in drinking water for 12 wk developed endothelial dysfunction, elevated BP, left-ventricular hypertrophy (Srivastava 2009).  
Mechanisms: Endothelial ROS, eNOS uncoupling, inflammatory cytokines, altered lipid metabolism.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  Confidence: high (consistent human & animal data).

4.2 Lymphatic / immune system  
Evidence  
– Human occupational studies: Smelter workers with urinary As >50 µg g-creatinine show ↓ peripheral CD4/CD8 ratios, reduced NK activity (Vahter 2007).  
– Children exposed via water (Bangladesh) exhibited lower vaccine-induced antibody titres (Ahmed 2011).  
– Rodent studies: 10 ppm NaAsO2 in drinking water for 30 d suppressed splenic lymphocyte proliferation and thymic weight (Kaltreider 2001).  
Predicted effects: Immunosuppression, impaired lymphopoiesis, possible lymphoid malignancies (limited, inconsistent).  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear immunotoxicity; direct lymphatic pathology less well documented).  Confidence: moderate (data gaps for chronic lymphatic disorders).

4.3 Respiratory system  
Evidence  
– Inhalation (smelters, pesticide formulators): ↑ bronchitis, chronic cough, reduced lung function; SMR for lung cancer 1.5–2.4 (Enterline 1995; Lubin 2008).  
– In vitro airway epithelium: AsIII (1–5 µM) induces mucin gene up-regulation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition.  
– Mouse inhalation (50 µg m-3, 6 h d-1, 13 wk): alveolar inflammation, fibrosis, and preneoplastic lesions (Tokar 2020).  
Mechanisms: ROS, DNA methylation changes, KRAS activation.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  Confidence: high (robust occupational & experimental evidence).

4.4 Digestive system  
Evidence  
– Acute oral exposure (>0.1 mg kg-bw) → abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, hemorrhagic gastroenteritis (WHO IPCS 2001).  
– Chronic ingestion (water 50–300 µg L-1): increased risk of hepatomegaly, portal hypertension, non-cirrhotic liver fibrosis (Mazumder 2003).  
– Animal data: Mice 25 ppm for 12 mo developed colonic barrier disruption and microbiota dysbiosis (Guo 2015).  
Mechanisms: Direct mucosal irritation, mitochondrial impairment in enterocytes, altered bile acid metabolism.  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH (severe acute GI toxicity; chronic hepatobiliary changes).  Confidence: moderate-to-high.

4.5 Urinary (renal & bladder) system  
Evidence  
– Epidemiology: Dose-dependent ↑ proteinuria, decreased eGFR in adults with urinary As >150 µg g-creatinine (Navas-Acien 2009).  
– Case–control (Taiwan): OR 2.1 for end-stage renal disease at water As>300 µg L-1 (Hsu 2017).  
– Repeated-dose rats (10 ppm, 90 d) show tubular necrosis, oxidative DNA damage; LOAEL ≈ 1 mg kg-bw d-1 (Lantz 2009).  
– Human carcinogenicity: Strong association with bladder cancer (IARC 2012).  
Mechanisms: Oxidative stress in proximal tubules, interference with thiol-dependent transporters, genotoxicity in urothelium.  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  Confidence: high (renal dysfunction and bladder carcinogenicity well documented).

5. Overall toxicological profile  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption by all relevant routes, slow clearance).  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH for cardiovascular, respiratory, and urinary systems; MEDIUM–HIGH for digestive; MEDIUM for lymphatic/immune.  
• Key hazards: Vascular disease, pulmonary disease including lung cancer, nephro-/uro-toxicity, immunosuppression, and multi-organ carcinogenesis.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Limited long-term controlled data on lymphatic malignancies.  
• Quantitative exposure-response below 10 µg L-1 in drinking water still debated.  
• Co-exposures (e.g., smoking, selenium status) modulate observed effects; need integrated models.

7. Risk-mitigating considerations  
• WHO guideline for arsenic in drinking water: 10 µg L-1 (health-based value unattainable in some regions; interim target 50 µg L-1).  
• Engineering controls for inhalation sources (smelters, glass manufacturing).  
• Biomonitoring (urinary inorganic+MMA+DMA) and methylation capacity assessment (MMA% as susceptibility marker).  
• Nutritional factors (folate, selenium) shown to improve arsenic methylation and reduce some endpoints, but are not substitutes for exposure reduction.

8. Conclusion  
Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6) exhibits high systemic availability and potent toxicodynamic activity. Human and animal evidence consistently demonstrate significant adverse effects on the cardiovascular, respiratory, and urinary systems, with moderate-to-high impacts on the digestive tract and measurable, though less conclusive, effects on the lymphatic/immune system. Preventive measures should aim at minimizing exposure, particularly via drinking water and occupational inhalation.

Only fragmentary information on systemic (non-neurological) toxicity of methylmercury is contained in the supplied material.  A weight-of-evidence evaluation drawn strictly from these excerpts is therefore limited and characterised by important data gaps.

1. Cardiovascular system
• No experimental animal or human studies that link methylmercury exposure to structural or functional cardiac changes are reported in the excerpts provided.  
• Weight-of-evidence judgement: Toxicodynamic activity – “low (no data)”; Systemic availability – “high” (organic mercury is well absorbed and distributed, but specific cardiovascular effects were not documented in these resources).  
Uncertainty: very high, because of the absence of direct data.

2. Lymphatic / haematopoietic system
• The excerpts do not describe alterations in lymph-node, spleen or immune parameters specifically attributable to methylmercury.  
• Weight-of-evidence judgement: Toxicodynamic activity – “low (no data)”; Systemic availability – “high”.  
Uncertainty: very high.

3. Respiratory system
• No respiratory-tract lesions or functional changes are reported for methylmercury in the material supplied.  
• Weight-of-evidence judgement: Toxicodynamic activity – “low (no data)”; Systemic availability – “high”.  
Uncertainty: very high.

4. Digestive system
• No direct gastrointestinal findings are described for methylmercury exposure in either animals or humans in the provided text.  
• Weight-of-evidence judgement: Toxicodynamic activity – “low (no data)”; Systemic availability – “high”.  
Uncertainty: very high.

5. Urinary (renal) system
• Rodent acute-toxicity comparison (Magas et al., 1985, cited in the resources) notes “renal damage greater for ethyl mercury”, implying that methylmercury also produces some renal injury, albeit less severe than ethylmercury.  
• Human clinical data (Hilmy et al., 1976) on ethyl mercury poisoning mention kidney involvement; however, no specific kidney findings are given for methylmercury.  
• These snippets nevertheless identify the kidney as a potential target.  
• Weight-of-evidence judgement: Toxicodynamic activity – “medium (limited animal indication of renal injury)”; Systemic availability – “high”.  
Uncertainty: high, owing to scarce quantitative detail.

Overall conclusion
Within the cardiovascular, lymphatic, respiratory, digestive and urinary systems, the only organ system for which the supplied references give any indication of adverse activity of methylmercury is the urinary (renal) system, and even that evidence is weak and indirect.  For all other systems, the present resources do not document specific effects.  Significant data gaps remain, and additional targeted studies or literature would be required to refine this assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Vinyl chloride  
CAS RN: 75-01-4  
Synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene, VCM  

Summary conclusion (weight of evidence)  
• Systemic availability: HIGH – rapidly absorbed by inhalation, highly lipophilic gas; extensive systemic distribution; hepatic first-pass metabolism produces reactive metabolites.  
• Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – well-documented genotoxicity and carcinogenicity; additional non-cancer organ toxicity in humans and laboratory animals.  

Below, the five organ systems requested are discussed separately. Strength of evidence, key studies, and data gaps are indicated.  

1. Cardiovascular system  
Known / observed effects  
• Occupational cohorts exposed to 5–100+ ppm vinyl chloride show a consistent excess of angiosarcoma of the liver (ASL). Although ASL occurs in the liver, it is a malignancy of vascular endothelial origin and therefore demonstrates specific toxicity to the cardiovascular/vascular endothelium (IARC, 2012; Ward et al., 2001).  
• Several cohort studies (Hagmar 1990; Mundt 2000) report increased incidence of Raynaud-like acro-osteolysis and scleroderma-type vascular disease in highly exposed workers, indicating micro-vascular injury.  
• Animal studies (rat, mouse: Maltoni 1981; Drew 1983) show increased incidence of hemangiosarcoma in multiple organs, suggesting a systemic vascular carcinogenic potential.  

Predicted / plausible effects  
• CYP2E1-mediated formation of chloroethylene oxide forms DNA adducts in endothelial cells → mutagenesis and clonal expansion of vascular tumours (Bolt 2010).  
• Metabolite-induced oxidative stress may promote atherosclerotic changes; limited epidemiology hints at elevated blood pressure but data are inconclusive.  

Toxicodynamic activity for cardiovascular system: HIGH  
Uncertainty: No clear signal for coronary heart disease or arrhythmia; data on blood pressure inconsistent.  

2. Lymphatic (immune/hematopoietic) system  
Known / observed effects  
• Several large PVC-worker studies report slight but reproducible elevations in non-Hodgkin lymphoma, multiple myeloma, and chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (Sathiakumar 2015; Mundt 2000). The SMRs are generally <2 and sometimes non-significant, so the association is considered limited.  
• ATSDR (2020) notes mild, transient leukopenia and thrombocytopenia in acutely over-exposed workers.  

Predicted / mechanistic support  
• Reactive metabolites form DNA–protein cross-links in bone-marrow cells (Bolt & Filser 1987).  
• Vinylidene chloride and 1,2-dichloroethane (structural analogues) are myelotoxic in rodents, supporting read-across to vinyl chloride.  

Toxicodynamic activity for lymphatic system: MEDIUM  
Uncertainty: Human data are suggestive but inconsistent; confounding co-exposures (e.g., styrene) common in workplaces.  

3. Respiratory system  
Known / observed effects  
• Acute high-level exposure (≥1,000 ppm) causes upper airway and pulmonary irritation, cough, and substernal chest pain; fatal pulmonary oedema reported in accidents (NIOSH 2015).  
• In long-term occupational studies, excess lung cancer has not been demonstrated after adjustment for smoking, but increased chronic bronchitis and impaired lung function (↓FEV1) have been noted in workers with >10 years exposure to >10 ppm (Dossing 1983; ATSDR 2020).  

Predicted / mechanistic support  
• Vinyl chloride itself is relatively unreactive, but its epoxide metabolite can be formed locally in airway tissue where CYP2E1 is expressed, potentially initiating epithelial dysplasia.  
• QSAR alerts: α-halogenated olefins are flagged for respiratory irritation sensitiser properties via Michael-type addition to nucleophiles (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.5).  

Toxicodynamic activity for respiratory system: MEDIUM (acute irritant, limited evidence for chronic structural damage)  
Uncertainty: Smoking is a major confounder; animal nose-only inhalation studies do not show strong lung pathology at concentrations causing liver tumours.  

4. Digestive system (with emphasis on liver)  
Known / observed effects  
• Liver: Strong, causal relationship with hepatic angiosarcoma (RR >100 in highly exposed PVC workers; IARC Group 1). Elevated hepatocellular carcinoma and cholangiocarcinoma also reported (IARC 2012; Boffetta 2014).  
• Non-cancer: occupational cohorts show increased liver enzyme activities (ALT, γ-GT), periportal fibrosis, and portal hypertension.  
• GI tract: limited evidence of stomach cancer increase; findings are inconsistent.  

Mechanism  
• Bioactivation in hepatocytes → chloroethylene oxide/chloroacetaldehyde → DNA adducts (etheno-dA, etheno-C) → mutagenesis.  
• Chronic activation of Kupffer cells and stellate cells leads to fibrosis (Csanády & Filser 2001).  

Toxicodynamic activity for digestive system: HIGH  
Uncertainty: Data on non-hepatic digestive organs (esophagus, colon) are scarce.  

5. Urinary system (kidney and lower tract)  
Known / observed effects  
• Some worker cohorts show slight but non-significant increases in mortality from renal cancer (Sathiakumar 2015); incidence studies generally null.  
• ATSDR cites case reports of mild proteinuria and decreased creatinine clearance after very high inhalational exposure, but not in typical workplace settings.  
• Rodent bioassays: increased renal tubular hyperplasia and rare renal cell tumours in male mice at ≥50 ppm (Drew 1983).  

Predicted / mechanistic support  
• Chloroacetaldehyde undergoes GSH conjugation; conjugates can deliver electrophilic fragments to the kidney (analogous to haloalkenes such as trichloroethylene), suggesting potential nephrotoxicity at high doses.  

Toxicodynamic activity for urinary system: LOW to MEDIUM (mainly at high, non-occupationally relevant doses)  
Uncertainty: Epidemiological evidence insufficient; co-exposure to other chlorinated solvents confounds interpretation.  

Systemic availability (ADME overview)  
• Absorption: >90 % of inhaled vinyl chloride retained at typical workplace concentrations; uptake also possible via skin from liquefied gas but is minor.  
• Distribution: Rapid distribution to all well-perfused tissues; fat:blood partition coefficient ≈3.  
• Metabolism: Predominantly hepatic CYP2E1 → reactive epoxide/alcohol metabolites; saturable above ~200 ppm.  
• Excretion: CO2 (exhaled) major, plus urinary thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA) conjugate; elimination t½ ≈24 h for metabolites.  
Given the high absorption and distribution, tissues in each organ system can be reached by parent compound or metabolites, supporting the “HIGH” systemic availability classification.  

Key references  
• IARC Monograph 100F (2012) Vinyl Chloride  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride (2020)  
• U.S. EPA IRIS (Update 2010)  
• Hagmar et al. Scand J Work Environ Health 1990;16:67-73.  
• Mundt et al. Occup Environ Med 2000;57:65-70.  
• Maltoni & Lefemine. Toxicology 1981;21:373-395.  
• Drew et al. J Natl Cancer Inst 1983;71:111-119.  

Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Limited high-quality data on kidney and immune disorders at low-to-moderate exposure levels.  
• Confounding co-exposures (styrene, dusts, phthalates) in PVC facilities may obscure respiratory and lymphatic outcomes.  
• No modern epidemiological studies evaluating cardiovascular endpoints such as ischemic heart disease in presently regulated exposure scenarios (<1 ppm).  

Overall hazard profile  
Vinyl chloride is a highly systemically available, metabolically activated, genotoxic gas. The strongest, well-substantiated human toxicities concern the digestive (liver) and vascular (cardiovascular) systems, with medium-strength evidence for immune/lymphatic and respiratory impacts and lower, less certain evidence for urinary tract effects.
